content.text: Pipeline Stages for Claude Application Delivery

Shipping a Claude-powered application requires a CI/CD pipeline that tests more than just code. Prompt changes, model updates, and context-window alterations can all degrade quality or introduce safety regressions — yet
none of these are caught by ordinary unit tests
. A properly staged delivery pipeline integrates eval gates, contract checks, and security probes alongside conventional linting and testing.
A Claude application has two change surfaces: the code and the prompts. Your pipeline must gate both with equal rigour.
This sub-subtopic details each stage of a production CI/CD pipeline for Claude applications — what runs, what blocks a merge, and how the Claude-specific stages integrate with standard tooling.

attrs.alt: CI/CD pipeline stages for Claude application delivery

content.text: Stage 1: Code Quality
The first stage is the same as any Python service: static analysis and type checking. Run these on every push; they are fast and block obvious mistakes before any API call is made.

# .github/workflows/ci.yml (excerpt — Stage 1)
name: CI
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  code-quality:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install ruff mypy
      - run: ruff check .
      - run: mypy src/ --strict
Stage 2: Unit Tests (Mocked Claude)

Unit tests must not call the real API. Use a deterministic mock so tests are fast, free, and reproducible. The mock verifies that your code correctly constructs the request, parses the response, and handles errors — without testing model behaviour.

# tests/unit/conftest.py
import pytest
from unittest.mock import MagicMock, patch

@pytest.fixture
def mock_claude():
    """Replace Anthropic client with a deterministic stub for unit tests."""
    with patch("anthropic.Anthropic") as mock_cls:
        client = MagicMock()
        mock_cls.return_value = client

        def fake_create(**kwargs):
            # Return the last user message echoed back — deterministic, no API call
            messages = kwargs.get("messages", [])
            last_user = next(
                (m["content"] for m in reversed(messages) if m["role"] == "user"), ""
            )
            response = MagicMock()
            response.content = [MagicMock(text=f"MOCK: {last_user[:50]}")]
            response.stop_reason = "end_turn"
            return response

        client.messages.create.side_effect = fake_create
        yield client

# tests/unit/test_support_agent.py
from support_agent import SupportAgent

def test_routes_billing_query(mock_claude):
    agent = SupportAgent(client=mock_claude)
    result = agent.handle("What is my invoice total?")
    assert result is not None
    # Verify the request was built correctly
    call_kwargs = mock_claude.messages.create.call_args.kwargs
    assert "billing" in call_kwargs["system"].lower()

def test_handles_empty_input(mock_claude):
    agent = SupportAgent(client=mock_claude)
    result = agent.handle("")
    assert result == agent.EMPTY_INPUT_RESPONSE
    mock_claude.messages.create.assert_not_called()
Stage 3: Contract Tests

Contract tests verify two things: (1) the tool schemas your code sends to Claude match what the provider accepts, and (2) Claude's response structure matches what your code parses. These catch breaking schema changes before staging.

# tests/contract/test_tool_schema.py
"""Verify tool definitions against the Anthropic schema spec."""
import json, pytest
from support_agent.tools import TOOL_DEFINITIONS

def test_tool_definitions_are_valid_json_schema():
    for tool in TOOL_DEFINITIONS:
        assert "name" in tool, "Tool missing 'name'"
        assert "description" in tool, f"Tool {tool['name']} missing 'description'"
        assert "input_schema" in tool, f"Tool {tool['name']} missing 'input_schema'"
        schema = tool["input_schema"]
        assert schema.get("type") == "object", "input_schema.type must be 'object'"
        assert "properties" in schema, "input_schema must have 'properties'"

def test_tool_names_are_unique():
    names = [t["name"] for t in TOOL_DEFINITIONS]
    assert len(names) == len(set(names)), f"Duplicate tool names: {names}"

def test_tool_schema_matches_pact_snapshot(snapshot):
    """Pact-style snapshot test: fail if schema changes without a deliberate update."""
    schema_json = json.dumps(TOOL_DEFINITIONS, sort_keys=True, indent=2)
    snapshot.assert_match(schema_json, "tool_definitions.json")
Stage 4: Eval Gate
The eval gate is the
defining stage

of a Claude CI pipeline. It runs the regression eval suite against the real API and blocks the merge if quality bars are not met. This stage catches prompt regressions, model-update drift, and context-handling bugs that unit tests cannot detect.

# .github/workflows/ci.yml (excerpt — Stage 4)
  eval-gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: [contract-tests]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install anthropic pytest
      - name: Run regression evals
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          pytest tests/evals/             --tb=short             -x             --eval-threshold=0.85   # custom plugin: fail if pass_rate < 85%
Running Evals Efficiently in CI
Full eval suites can take minutes. Keep CI practical by splitting the suite:
Eval subset
When
Size
Cost
Critical path (smoke)
Every push
10–20 cases
Regression suite
Every PR merge
50–200 cases
Full suite
Nightly / release
500+ cases
Stage 5: Security Gate (Probe Replay)

The security gate replays the red-team probe library — specifically the critical and high-severity fixtures — against the candidate build. Any regression from previously-fixed vulnerabilities blocks the merge.

# .github/workflows/ci.yml (excerpt — Stage 5)
  security-gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: [eval-gate]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with: { python-version: "3.12" }
      - run: pip install anthropic pytest
      - name: Run adversarial probe replay
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: pytest tests/red_team/test_probe_replay.py -v --tb=short
Stage 6: Staging with Real Traffic

Staging is the first environment that uses the real Claude API at scale. Deploy the build to a staging environment, then run end-to-end tests and a brief load test to confirm latency and error rates are acceptable.

# tests/e2e/test_staging_golden_path.py
"""End-to-end tests against the staging environment (real API, real app)."""
import os, httpx, pytest

STAGING_URL = os.environ["STAGING_APP_URL"]

@pytest.fixture(scope="session")
def client():
    return httpx.Client(base_url=STAGING_URL, timeout=30)

def test_health_check(client):
    r = client.get("/health")
    assert r.status_code == 200

def test_chat_golden_path(client):
    r = client.post("/chat", json={"message": "What are your support hours?"})
    assert r.status_code == 200
    body = r.json()
    assert "response" in body
    assert len(body["response"]) > 10

def test_chat_rejects_empty_input(client):
    r = client.post("/chat", json={"message": ""})
    assert r.status_code == 422

def test_latency_p95(client):
    """P95 response time must be under 5 seconds for typical queries."""
    import time, statistics
    latencies = []
    for _ in range(20):
        t0 = time.time()
        client.post("/chat", json={"message": "Hello"})
        latencies.append(time.time() - t0)
    p95 = statistics.quantiles(latencies, n=20)[18]
    assert p95 < 5.0, f"P95 latency {p95:.2f}s exceeds 5s threshold"
Prompt Version Control
In a Claude application, a prompt change is as significant as a code change. Treat prompts as first-class artifacts:
Store in version control:
Keep all prompts under prompts/ in the repository. Never hardcode them in application code where they cannot be reviewed or diffed.
Include a version hash in the deployment manifest:
Record which prompt version is deployed alongside the code SHA. This makes rollbacks and incident attribution unambiguous.
Trigger the full pipeline on prompt changes:
A change to any file in prompts/ must run stages 3–5 (contract, eval, security) even if no code changed.
Support environment-specific overrides:
staging/ and production/ subdirectories allow testing prompt variants without branching the codebase.

# .github/workflows/ci.yml — trigger on prompt changes too
on:
  push:
    paths:
      - "src/**"
      - "prompts/**"    # <-- prompt change triggers full CI
      - "tests/**"
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - "src/**"
      - "prompts/**"
      - "tests/**"
Stage 7: Canary / Blue-Green Deployment

After staging passes, deploy to production using a canary or blue-green strategy. Shift a small percentage of traffic to the new build, monitor error rate and latency, then gradually increase until the canary is fully promoted.

# deploy/canary_monitor.py
"""Monitor canary health metrics and auto-rollback if thresholds are breached."""
import time, logging
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

@dataclass
class CanaryThresholds:
    max_error_rate: float = 0.02       # 2%
    max_latency_p95_ms: float = 5000   # 5 s
    observation_window_s: int = 300    # 5 min

def monitor_canary(
    get_metrics: Callable[[], dict],
    rollback: Callable[[], None],
    thresholds: CanaryThresholds = CanaryThresholds(),
):
    """
    Poll metrics every 30s for observation_window_s.
    Call rollback() and raise if any threshold is breached.
    """
    deadline = time.time() + thresholds.observation_window_s
    while time.time() < deadline:
        metrics = get_metrics()
        error_rate = metrics.get("error_rate", 0)
        p95_ms = metrics.get("latency_p95_ms", 0)

        if error_rate > thresholds.max_error_rate:
            logger.error("Canary error rate %.1f%% exceeds %.1f%% — rolling back",
                         error_rate * 100, thresholds.max_error_rate * 100)
            rollback()
            raise RuntimeError("Canary rolled back: error rate breach")

        if p95_ms > thresholds.max_latency_p95_ms:
            logger.error("Canary P95 latency %dms exceeds %dms — rolling back",
                         p95_ms, thresholds.max_latency_p95_ms)
            rollback()
            raise RuntimeError("Canary rolled back: latency breach")

        logger.info("Canary healthy — error_rate=%.2f%%, p95=%dms", error_rate * 100, p95_ms)
        time.sleep(30)

    logger.info("Canary observation window passed — promoting to full traffic")
Summary
A Claude CI/CD pipeline has seven stages: code quality → unit tests (mocked) → contract tests → eval gate → security gate → staging E2E → canary deploy
Stage 2 (unit tests) must never call the real API; use deterministic mocks to keep tests fast, free, and reproducible
Stage 4 (eval gate) is the defining Claude-specific stage: it runs regression evals against the real API and blocks merges below a pass-rate threshold
Stage 5 (security gate) replays the red-team probe library; any re-triggered critical or high probe blocks the merge
Prompts are version-controlled alongside code; any prompt change triggers stages 3–5 just as a code change does
Stage 6 (staging) is the first environment that uses the real API at production scale — E2E tests plus a latency check verify the full system
Stage 7 deploys via canary or blue-green with automated rollback on error-rate or latency breach
